business_info: A standard pit stop is defined as a service that is completed within the optimal duration window expected for the specific track conditions.

Teams internally categorize pit lane work into "service stops" (tyres, wing, minor checks) and "reset stops" (including steering wheel swaps and full systems reset), but the database pitStops table does not distinguish between these categories: all are simply logged as pit stops with a duration in milliseconds.

Some race engineers will refer to a "pseudo-stop" when the driver briefly enters the pit lane to serve a penalty or drive-through without tyre service being performed; these events are still recorded in pitStops with a duration, even though no new tyres are fitted.

Operational dashboards sometimes show a "pit delta" figure, which is the total time loss for a pit stop sequence (pit-in to pit-out), but the pitStops.durationMs field in the database only represents the stationary time in the box and not the full delta across the pit lane.

The sporting group uses the phrase "strategic stop" for pit stops that are planned in advance rather than reactions to incidents; there is no strategic flag in the pitStops table, so this concept is reconstructed later by strategy analysts using lap timing data.

In debrief notes, strategists sometimes distinguish between an "offset stop" (pitting earlier than the main field) and an "overcut stop" (pitting later and pushing on worn tyres), but both of these are captured identically in pitStops without any offset/overcut indicator.

The circuits table includes some venues that have undergone significant layout changes over time; for historical analysis, analysts sometimes treat pre- and post-redesign layouts as separate "eras" of the same circuit even though they share a single circuitId in the database.

Internal sporting documents occasionally refer to "heritage" circuits, a loose, narrative-driven category used by marketing to highlight traditional venues. This marketing usage does not correspond to any field or flag in the circuits table and is not applied consistently across seasons.

Some tracks that hosted non-championship or junior category events are included in circuits for legacy reasons even if they never held a Formula 1 World Championship race; analysts typically filter on the races table when they want the official F1 calendar only.

When marketing describes a Grand Prix as "classic" or "iconic", it is usually based on historical narratives and fan sentiment, not on a rulebook definition, and this label is not stored in either the circuits or races tables.

The constructorResults table can be joined to constructors and races to approximate team performance trends, but internal sporting reviews often reweight these results to account for disqualifications or post-race penalties that may not be reflected in the raw points column.

In some internal reports, analysts create a "cleaned constructor score" that manually removes points earned in races with major technical infringements, but this adjusted score is not stored in the constructorResults table and is recomputed ad hoc in reporting tools.

When reviewing performance at a race weekend level, sporting management may focus on the constructorStandings table rather than constructorResults, because standings already incorporate series-level context like cumulative points and rank.

Some legacy analytics code computes an internal "constructor efficiency" metric as total constructorResults.points divided by the number of race starts for that constructor, but this is only exposed in BI dashboards and is not stored as a column in the warehouse.

Constructor-related KPIs in management packs often distinguish between "scored points" from official classifications and "performance-adjusted points" that normalize for retirements outside the team’s control; both metrics are derived from constructorResults but use different weighting schemes.

On some weekends with sprint races, internal tools sometimes attribute a composite constructor score to the main Grand Prix that combines sprint and race outcomes; this composite score is calculated in reporting only and does not appear as a standalone field in constructorResults.

Where a constructor has been excluded from a race after the fact, constructorStandings may show a different cumulative points total than what you would get by naively summing constructorResults.points, because standings reflect the official ruling effective at that time.

Race strategy reviews sometimes tag certain events as "phantom stops" when a driver is called into the pits but then stays out; these calls leave a trace in radio transcripts but, by definition, never appear in the pitStops table because no actual stop occurred.

In some historical seasons, mid-race refuelling stops were logged in the same way as tyre stops; the pitStops.durationMs values during those eras may not be directly comparable to modern non-refuelling stops because the operational sequence was longer and more complex.

Some teams internally flag a "scrub stop" where the main purpose is to switch to scrubbed tyres for balance reasons; this nuance is not recorded in the warehouse, where all pitStops are treated uniformly.

Post-race reliability reports sometimes correlate pitStops with lapTimes to identify "slow out-laps" after tyre changes, but this analysis is performed in notebooks and BI tools rather than being expressed as a dedicated field in the underlying database.

In wet or mixed-weather races, the definition of a "tyre phase" is often based on stretches of laps between pitStops where the car stays on a given compound; these phases are derived analytically from lapTimes and pitStops and are not present in any single table.

The drivers table occasionally includes placeholder entries for test or reserve drivers who never actually started a Grand Prix; these drivers will not appear in results for official races but may appear in historical or testing-related datasets elsewhere.

Some analytics teams compute a derived "driver stint" table, where a stint is defined as the continuous sequence of laps between two pitStops events for a given driver in a race; this table is not part of the core schema and is regenerated for each analysis.

Qualifying sessions are stored in the qualifying table and not directly linked to constructorResults; internal weekend summaries sometimes merge qualifying and race metrics into a single "weekend score", but that aggregation logic lives only in reporting code.

The seasons table is purely a lookup for years and URLs; regardless of how a season is branded commercially (e.g., "Hybrid Era"), those marketing labels are not recorded in the database and should not be used for filtering data.

Internal sporting regulations sometimes distinguish between a "World Championship" race and non-championship demonstration events, but in this warehouse the races table only includes official World Championship Grands Prix.

Some venues in the circuits table have hosted races under different event titles (e.g., European GP, Grand Prix of [region]); marketing may treat these as distinct events, but analytically they are tied together through the shared circuitId.

The nationality fields in constructors and drivers are based on sporting nationality, which may differ from company registration country or driver birthplace; consistency across tables is not enforced beyond the stored text values.
